hypothesis: Replacing the template-based natural language prompt generation with prompts authored by domain-naive human users will reveal a systematic degradation in CHROMA's color accuracy (increased mean ∆E) that correlates specifically with the use of ambiguous perceptual color descriptors (e.g., "warm," "muted," "vivid") rather than with structural constraint complexity, indicating that the frozen LLM encoder's projection to a fixed 512-dimensional bottleneck fails to preserve the fine-grained colorimetric distinctions encoded in colloquial color language, and that fine-tuning only the encoder projection layer on a small set of human-authored prompt–spectrum pairs will recover performance more efficiently than increasing decoder depth.
